questions.question:
Suppose a database consists of the following relations:
SUPPLIER (SCODE, SNAME, CITY)
PART (PCODE, PNAME, PDESC, CITY)
PROJECTS (PRCODE, PRNAME, PRCITY)
SPPR (SCODE, PCODE, PRCODE, QTY)

Write algebraic (relational algebra) solutions to the following:
i. Get SCODE values for suppliers who supply to both projects PR1 and PR2.
ii. Get PCODE values for parts supplied by a supplier in the first city to a project in the second city, but do not print PCODE values where the two cities are the same.
questions.answer:
i. pi_{SCODE}(sigma_{PRCODE='PR1'}(SPPR)) INTERSECT pi_{SCODE}(sigma_{PRCODE='PR2'}(SPPR))
ii. pi_{PCODE}(sigma_{SUPPLIER.CITY != PROJECTS.PRCITY}(SPPR JOIN SUPPLIER JOIN PROJECTS))
questions.to_find: Relational algebra expressions for two queries on the SUPPLIER/PART/PROJECTS/SPPR schema

understand.plain: We have four relations: SUPPLIER (who supplies), PART (what is supplied), PROJECTS (to whom it is supplied), and SPPR (the supply relationship linking supplier, part, project, and quantity). The first query finds suppliers who supply to BOTH project PR1 AND project PR2. The second query finds parts that are shipped from a supplier in one city to a project in a different city.

keywords.explain: A formal query language for relational databases using operations like select (sigma), project (pi), join, union, intersection, and difference.
keywords.example: pi_{SCODE}(SPPR) returns the set of all SCODE values in the SPPR relation.

keywords.explain: Combines two relations on their common attributes, keeping only tuples that match on those attributes.
keywords.example: SPPR JOIN SUPPLIER combines on SCODE, giving each supply record the supplier's name and city.

keywords.explain: Returns only tuples that appear in BOTH operand relations (both must have the same schema).
keywords.example: R INTERSECT S gives rows present in both R and S.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='150' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='85' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SUPPLIER</text><text x='85' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>SCODE,SNAME,CITY</text><rect x='180' y='10' width='150' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='255' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>PART</text><text x='255' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>PCODE,PNAME,CITY</text><rect x='350' y='10' width='155' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='427' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>PROJECTS</text><text x='427' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>PRCODE,PRNAME,PRCITY</text><rect x='175' y='120' width='165' height='70' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='258' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SPPR</text><text x='258' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>SCODE,PCODE,PRCODE,QTY</text><line x1='85' y1='70' x2='230' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='255' y1='70' x2='255' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='427' y1='70' x2='290' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four relation boxes: SUPPLIER, PART, PROJECTS all connected down into the central SPPR relation which links them via SCODE, PCODE, PRCODE and QTY.

given.aim: Write relational algebra expressions for two supplier/project queries.

terms.meaning: Supply relation storing which supplier (SCODE) supplies which part (PCODE) to which project (PRCODE) in what quantity (QTY).
terms.example: A tuple (S1, P3, PR1, 100) means supplier S1 supplies 100 units of part P3 to project PR1.
terms.connects: The primary relation used in both queries to identify supply relationships.

terms.meaning: Selection operation that filters SPPR to only rows where PRCODE = 'PR1'.
terms.example: Returns all supply records for project PR1.
terms.connects: Used in part (i) to separately identify suppliers of PR1 and suppliers of PR2.

terms.meaning: Set intersection — returns only SCODEs that appear in BOTH selected sets.
terms.example: If PR1 suppliers = {S1,S2,S3} and PR2 suppliers = {S2,S3,S4}, intersection = {S2,S3}.
terms.connects: Finds suppliers who supply to BOTH projects.

terms.meaning: A theta-join filter that keeps only supply triples where the supplier's city differs from the project's city.
terms.example: If supplier is in Delhi and project is in Mumbai, the tuple is included.
terms.connects: Implements the 'two different cities' condition in part (ii).

given.plan: For part (i): project SCODE from SPPR filtered to PR1, project SCODE from SPPR filtered to PR2, then intersect both sets. For part (ii): join SPPR with SUPPLIER and PROJECTS, apply the condition that SUPPLIER.CITY != PROJECTS.PRCITY, then project PCODE.

steps.title: Part (i) — Suppliers supplying to both PR1 and PR2
steps.apply:
Step A: sigma_{PRCODE='PR1'}(SPPR) selects all rows in SPPR where PRCODE = 'PR1'.
Step B: pi_{SCODE}(...) projects just the SCODE column, giving the set of suppliers for PR1.
Step C: Repeat for PR2.
Step D: INTERSECT the two SCODE sets — only suppliers appearing in both sets are kept.
steps.note: Intersection naturally enforces the 'both' condition. A supplier appearing in only one set is excluded.

steps.title: Part (ii) — Parts shipped across different cities
steps.formula_raw: pi_{PCODE}(sigma_{SUPPLIER.CITY != PROJECTS.PRCITY}(SPPR JOIN SUPPLIER JOIN PROJECTS))
steps.apply:
Step A: Join SPPR with SUPPLIER on SCODE — adds SUPPLIER.CITY to each supply tuple.
Step B: Join the result with PROJECTS on PRCODE — adds PROJECTS.PRCITY to each tuple.
Step C: Apply sigma_{SUPPLIER.CITY != PROJECTS.PRCITY} to filter out same-city pairs.
Step D: Project PCODE to get only the part codes.
steps.note: The natural join on SCODE and PRCODE correctly links each supply record to its supplier's city and its project's city.

solution.result:
i. pi_{SCODE}(sigma_{PRCODE='PR1'}(SPPR)) INTERSECT pi_{SCODE}(sigma_{PRCODE='PR2'}(SPPR))
ii. pi_{PCODE}(sigma_{SUPPLIER.CITY != PROJECTS.PRCITY}(SPPR JOIN SUPPLIER JOIN PROJECTS))

questions.formula_note: Relational algebra: selection (sigma), projection (pi), natural join, and set intersection. The 'supply to both projects' pattern uses intersection of projected selections.

questions.question:
Suppose a database consists of the following relations:
SUPPLIER (SCODE, SNAME, CITY)
PART (PCODE, PNAME, PDESC, CITY)
PROJECTS (PRCODE, PRNAME, PRCITY)
SPPR (SCODE, PCODE, PRCODE, QTY)

Write SQL programs corresponding to the following queries:
i. Print PCODE values for parts supplied to any project in DELHI by a supplier in DELHI.
ii. Print all triples <CITY, PCODE, CITY> such that a supplier in the first city supplies the specified part to a project in the second city, but do not print the triples in which the two CITY values are the same.

Which of the following SQL queries correctly answers query (i)?
A. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'
B. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR WHERE SCODE IN (SELECT SCODE FROM SUPPLIER WHERE CITY = 'DELHI')
C. SELECT PCODE FROM PART WHERE CITY = 'DELHI'
D. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND PRCITY = 'DELHI'
questions.answer: A. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'
questions.to_find: SQL query to retrieve PCODE values for parts supplied to any project in DELHI by a supplier also in DELHI

understand.plain: We need to find all part codes (PCODE) where both the supplier city AND the project city are 'DELHI'. This requires joining three tables: SPPR (the supply relationship), SUPPLIER (to check supplier city), and PROJECTS (to check project city). Both conditions must hold simultaneously.

keywords.explain: Linking two tables via a common attribute to combine information from both.
keywords.example: SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE links each supply record to the corresponding supplier's details.

keywords.explain: Using WHERE clause conditions to restrict which rows are returned.
keywords.example: SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI' keeps only rows where both cities are Delhi.

understand.visual_alt: A three-way join of SPPR, SUPPLIER, and PROJECTS filtered by SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI', then projecting PCODE.

given.aim: Find SQL query that correctly retrieves part codes supplied to Delhi projects by Delhi suppliers.

terms.meaning: Join condition linking each supply record to the supplying company.
terms.example: Ensures we get the supplier's CITY attribute for the supplier of this part.

terms.meaning: Join condition linking each supply record to the destination project.
terms.example: Ensures we get the project's PRCITY attribute.

terms.meaning: The dual condition that both the supplier and the project must be in Delhi.
terms.example: A supply from supplier in Delhi to project in Delhi is included; one from Delhi to Mumbai is not.
terms.connects: Enforces both constraints of the query simultaneously.

given.plan: Join SPPR with SUPPLIER (on SCODE) and PROJECTS (on PRCODE), then apply both city filters and project PCODE. Evaluate each option to see which does this correctly.

steps.title: Analyze Option A
steps.formula_raw: SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'
steps.apply: This performs a three-way join of SPPR, SUPPLIER, and PROJECTS with proper join conditions and both city filters. It correctly retrieves PCODE for parts supplied by a Delhi supplier to a Delhi project.
steps.note: This is the correct and complete query.

steps.title: Analyze Options B, C, D
steps.apply:
Option B: Only checks supplier city = DELHI, ignores project city condition — incomplete.
Option C: Checks PART.CITY = 'DELHI' which is the city where the part is stocked, not supplier/project city — wrong interpretation.
Option D: Only joins SPPR with PROJECTS, checks project city but ignores supplier city condition — incomplete.
steps.note: Only Option A satisfies both the supplier-in-Delhi AND project-in-Delhi conditions.

steps.title: SQL for Part (ii) — triples with different cities
steps.formula_raw: SELECT SUPPLIER.CITY, SPPR.PCODE, PROJECTS.PRCITY FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY <> PROJECTS.PRCITY
steps.apply: Join SPPR with SUPPLIER and PROJECTS; filter where SUPPLIER.CITY != PROJECTS.PRCITY; select the triple <SUPPLIER.CITY, PCODE, PROJECTS.PRCITY>.

solution.result: A. SELECT PCODE FROM SPPR, SUPPLIER, PROJECTS WHERE SPPR.SCODE = SUPPLIER.SCODE AND SPPR.PRCODE = PROJECTS.PRCODE AND SUPPLIER.CITY = 'DELHI' AND PROJECTS.PRCITY = 'DELHI'

questions.formula_note: SQL three-way equi-join with dual predicate filtering. Both join conditions (SCODE match, PRCODE match) and both city conditions must be present for correctness.

questions.question:
Suppose we have a database consisting of the following three relations:
- FREQUENTS (student, parlor) giving the parlors each student visits.
- SERVES (parlor, ice-cream) indicating what kind of ice-creams each parlor serves.
- LIKES (student, ice-cream) indicating what ice-creams each student likes.

(Assume that each student likes at least one ice-cream and frequents at least one parlor.)

Express the following in SQL:
Find the students that frequent at least one parlor that serves some ice-cream that they like.

Which of the following is correct?
A. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
B. SELECT student FROM LIKES WHERE ice-cream IN (SELECT ice-cream FROM SERVES)
C. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS WHERE parlor IN (SELECT parlor FROM SERVES)
D. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
questions.answer: A. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
questions.to_find: SQL query to find students who frequent at least one parlor serving at least one ice-cream they like

understand.plain: We have three tables: FREQUENTS tells us which parlors each student visits, SERVES tells us which ice-creams each parlor offers, and LIKES tells us which ice-creams each student enjoys. We want students where there is at least one (parlor, ice-cream) pair such that the student frequents that parlor, the parlor serves that ice-cream, AND the student likes that ice-cream. All three conditions must simultaneously hold.

keywords.explain: Combining three tables so that records from all three match on shared attributes.
keywords.example: Joining FREQUENTS, SERVES, and LIKES on parlor (FREQUENTS-SERVES) and student+ice-cream (SERVES-LIKES) finds matching (student, parlor, ice-cream) triples.

keywords.explain: A query asking 'does there EXIST at least one tuple satisfying some condition?' — answered by a join or a subquery with IN/EXISTS.
keywords.example: Finding students who frequent at least one 'good' parlor is existential — we just need one qualifying row.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='70' width='140' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='80' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>FREQUENTS</text><text x='80' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>student, parlor</text><rect x='170' y='10' width='140' height='60' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='240' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>SERVES</text><text x='240' y='58' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>parlor, ice-cream</text><rect x='330' y='70' width='140' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='400' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>LIKES</text><text x='400' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>student, ice-cream</text><line x1='150' y1='100' x2='170' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='310' y1='60' x2='330' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='158' y='80' class='sub mut'>parlor</text><text x='295' y='80' class='sub mut'>ice-cream</text><line x1='80' y1='130' x2='400' y2='130' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,4'/><text x='240' y='162' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>student (shared)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three tables: FREQUENTS and SERVES share 'parlor', SERVES and LIKES share 'ice-cream', FREQUENTS and LIKES share 'student'. The query needs all three links to hold simultaneously.

given.aim: Find all students for whom there exists a parlor they frequent that serves an ice-cream they like.

terms.meaning: Links a student's visited parlor to the parlors in the SERVES table.
terms.example: If student Alice frequents 'Creamery', then SERVES rows for 'Creamery' are joined in.
terms.connects: Brings the ice-creams available at Alice's parlor into scope.

terms.meaning: Links the ice-creams served at a parlor to the ice-creams a student likes.
terms.example: If 'Creamery' serves 'Vanilla' and Alice likes 'Vanilla', these rows join.
terms.connects: Ensures the ice-cream served is one the student actually likes.

terms.meaning: Ensures the student in FREQUENTS (who visits the parlor) is the same student in LIKES (who likes the ice-cream).
terms.example: Prevents mixing Alice's parlor visits with Bob's ice-cream preferences.
terms.connects: Ties the three conditions to the same individual student.

given.plan: Join FREQUENTS, SERVES, and LIKES on the three shared attributes (parlor, ice-cream, student). Any student appearing in the result frequents a parlor that serves an ice-cream they like. Project the student column (use DISTINCT to avoid duplicates).

steps.title: Understand what the query needs
steps.apply: We need a student s such that: THERE EXISTS a parlor p and an ice-cream i where (s, p) in FREQUENTS AND (p, i) in SERVES AND (s, i) in LIKES. This is a three-way join on three attributes.
steps.note: All three conditions must be satisfied by the SAME (student, parlor, ice-cream) combination.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A — three-way join
steps.formula_raw: SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student
steps.apply: This query joins all three tables with all three required conditions. Every row in the result corresponds to a valid (student, parlor, ice-cream) triple. Projecting 'student' gives exactly the students who satisfy the existential requirement.
steps.note: The attribute name 'ice-cream' with a hyphen may need quoting in some SQL dialects, but the logic is correct.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply:
Option B: Only checks if the student likes an ice-cream that some parlor serves — does not check if the student frequents THAT parlor.
Option C: Only checks if the student frequents a parlor that serves something — does not check if what is served is something the student likes.
Option D: Joins FREQUENTS and LIKES only — no connection to SERVES, so no check on what the parlor actually serves.
steps.note: Options B, C, and D each miss at least one of the three required conditions.

solution.result: A. SELECT student FROM FREQUENTS, SERVES, LIKES WHERE FREQUENTS.parlor = SERVES.parlor AND SERVES.ice-cream = LIKES.ice-cream AND FREQUENTS.student = LIKES.student

questions.formula_note: Three-way natural/equi-join in SQL to answer an existential query. All three shared attributes (parlor, ice-cream, student) must be equated across the three relations to correctly capture the 'frequents a parlor that serves a liked ice-cream' condition.
